enrichment.short_description: Domo is a cloud-based business intelligence platform that connects to hundreds of data sources to build real-time dashboards, data apps, and AI-driven analytics for entire organizations.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Domo is a cloud BI platform with real-time dashboards, 1,000+ connectors, and AI analytics. See 2026 credit-based pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Domo is a cloud-native business intelligence platform that lets organizations connect, transform, visualize, and act on data from hundreds of sources inside a single, mobile-friendly workspace. Rather than requiring separate ETL tools, a data warehouse, and a BI front end, Domo bundles data integration (via prebuilt connectors and its Workbench and Magic ETL tools), a governed cloud data warehouse (Domo's Adrenaline engine), interactive dashboards (DomoBoards), low-code data apps (App Studio), and embedded analytics (Domo Everywhere) into one product. In recent years the company has layered generative and agentic AI features under the Domo.AI umbrella, including natural-language querying, AI-generated summaries of dashboards, and automated data-story narration, positioning Domo as an end-to-end analytics platform rather than a pure visualization tool.

The company was founded in 2010 by Josh James, who had previously co-founded and sold the web-analytics company Omniture to Adobe for roughly $1.8 billion. James built Domo largely out of the public eye for several years before its 2015 general launch, then took the company public on the NASDAQ under the ticker DOMO in June 2018 at $21 per share. Domo is headquartered in American Fork, Utah, part of the state's 'Silicon Slopes' tech corridor, and employs roughly 800-900 people as a publicly traded software company. Domo's early positioning emphasized executive-friendly, mobile-first dashboards that could be consumed on a phone as easily as a desktop, a differentiator from the more analyst-centric tools of the era like Tableau and QlikView.

Technically, Domo operates as a fully managed SaaS platform: customers do not install or maintain infrastructure. Data lands in Domo through more than 1,000 pre-built connectors (databases, cloud apps, spreadsheets, APIs, and streaming sources), gets transformed using either a visual drag-and-drop ETL canvas (Magic ETL) or SQL-based data flows, and is stored in Domo's cloud data warehouse for fast querying. From there, users build dashboards and 'Domo Apps' using a library of visualization widgets, or expose curated views to external users and customers through Domo Everywhere, an embedding and white-labeling framework. Domo also supports a governance layer (PDP - Personalized Data Permissions) so different users or embedded audiences only see rows of data relevant to them, which is a common requirement for analytics that get resold or shared with clients.

Domo's pricing has changed significantly over its history. The company originally sold fixed per-user subscription tiers, but since 2023 it has moved to a consumption-based credit model: customers commit to an annual or multi-year contract for a pool of credits, and usage of data-related operations (rows processed, queries run, data refreshes, AI features, etc.) draws down those credits. All core platform features and unlimited users are technically available from day one; what customers pay for is consumption rather than named seats, though Domo still differentiates three license types for governance purposes - Privileged users (who can build and edit content), Participant users (who primarily view and interact with dashboards), and Social users (limited, lightweight access). Domo does not publish fixed price lists; prospective customers get a custom quote, and independent analyses of real customer contracts suggest a practical floor of roughly $30,000 per year for a viable deployment, with effective per-user costs that can run from under $100 to several hundred dollars per user per month depending on contract size and usage intensity. A free 30-day trial with full features and unlimited users is available without requiring a credit card, giving prospective buyers a way to evaluate the platform before entering a paid negotiation.

Domo is aimed primarily at mid-market and enterprise organizations that want a single, IT-light platform to centralize reporting across sales, marketing, operations, and finance, and that value the ability to embed live analytics into customer-facing products or portals. Retail, healthcare, financial services, and franchise-style multi-location businesses are common Domo customers because the platform's real-time refresh and mobile dashboards suit operational monitoring as much as historical reporting. It competes most directly with Tableau (owned by Salesforce), Microsoft Power BI, Google Looker, and Qlik Sense, as well as embedded-analytics specialists like Sisense. Domo differentiates itself by owning more of the stack out of the box - connectors, ETL, warehouse, visualization, app-building, and embedding - so customers do not need to separately license and integrate a data warehouse or ETL tool, in contrast to Power BI's typical dependence on Azure services or Tableau's reliance on external data prep tools. The tradeoff commonly cited by reviewers is that Domo's opaque, consumption-based pricing makes total cost of ownership harder to forecast than competitors with transparent per-user pricing, and that Domo can become expensive at scale compared with Power BI in particular.

Notable facts about Domo include its founder's history building and selling Omniture, its NASDAQ public listing since 2018, its Utah headquarters in the Silicon Slopes tech hub, and its shift away from a traditional per-seat SaaS pricing model toward a credit-based consumption system that is relatively unusual among mainstream BI vendors. The company continues to invest heavily in AI features, integrating large language model-powered chat and summarization directly into dashboards so that non-technical business users can ask questions of their data in natural language rather than building charts manually.

Domo's product history reflects several distinct eras. In its earliest years the company operated largely in stealth, refining the core dashboard and connector experience before its 2015 public launch, and it built an early reputation on 'Domo Buzz,' a social-collaboration layer that let teams comment and chat directly alongside data visualizations, an unusual pairing of BI and messaging for its time. As the product matured, the company shifted emphasis toward openness and extensibility, publishing APIs and SDKs that let customers and partners build custom Domo Apps, and investing in Domo.AI and its Text to SQL and AI chat capabilities to keep pace with the broader industry move toward conversational analytics. Domo has also pursued industry-specific packaging, offering pre-built app templates and connectors tailored to retail, healthcare, and financial services workflows so customers in those verticals can get to a working dashboard faster than building entirely from scratch.

On review platforms such as G2 and Capterra, Domo consistently draws praise for its ease of use, visual polish, and mobile experience relative to more analyst-oriented tools, while its most common criticism across independent buyer guides and Reddit/community discussions is the difficulty of predicting total cost once a contract moves from trial to production scale. Buyers considering Domo alongside Power BI or Tableau are typically weighing that ease-of-use and all-in-one convenience against Microsoft and Salesforce's larger partner ecosystems, broader enterprise licensing bundles (such as Power BI's inclusion in Microsoft 365 E5), and more transparent, publicly listed per-user pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Domo is a cloud-native business intelligence platform that unifies data integration, a managed data warehouse, dashboarding, low-code app building, and AI-assisted analytics in a single product. It was built to let executives and operational teams see up-to-date metrics on any device, not just data analysts working in a desktop BI tool.

Founded in 2010 by Josh James (previously the co-founder of Omniture) and headquartered in American Fork, Utah, Domo has been publicly traded on the NASDAQ under the ticker DOMO since its 2018 IPO. The company positions itself as an all-in-one alternative to stitching together separate ETL, warehouse, and visualization tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Domo ships with more than 1,000 pre-built data connectors covering databases, cloud applications, spreadsheets, and APIs, along with Magic ETL, a visual drag-and-drop data transformation canvas that does not require SQL knowledge.

Domo Everywhere lets organizations embed live, white-labeled dashboards into customer-facing products or portals, while App Studio provides low-code tools for building interactive data apps on top of governed datasets. Domo.AI adds natural-language querying and automated narrative summaries on top of dashboards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Since 2023, Domo has priced the platform using a consumption-based credit model rather than fixed per-user tiers: customers commit to an annual or multi-year contract for a pool of credits that is drawn down by data-related usage, with all users and core features included from day one.

Domo does not publish list prices; every paid deployment is custom-quoted, and independent analyses of customer contracts put the realistic entry point at roughly $30,000 per year. A free 30-day full-featured trial with unlimited users is available without a credit card.

faqs.question: Is Domo free to use?
faqs.answer: Domo is not permanently free, but it offers a 30-day full-access trial with unlimited users and no credit card required. Ongoing use requires a paid, custom-quoted contract.

faqs.question: How does Domo pricing work?
faqs.answer: Domo uses a consumption-based credit model. Customers commit to a pool of credits through an annual or multi-year contract, and credits are consumed based on platform usage such as data processing and refreshes, rather than a fixed price per named user.

faqs.question: Who founded Domo?
faqs.answer: Domo was founded in 2010 by Josh James, who previously co-founded and sold the web-analytics company Omniture to Adobe.

faqs.question: Is Domo a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes. Domo, Inc. has traded on the NASDAQ under the ticker DOMO since its June 2018 initial public offering.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Domo?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Tableau, Microsoft Power BI, Google Looker, and Qlik Sense, along with embedded-analytics specialists such as Sisense.

faqs.question: Can Domo be embedded into another product?
faqs.answer: Yes. Domo Everywhere is a dedicated embedding and white-labeling framework that lets companies surface live Domo dashboards inside their own customer-facing applications.

enrichment.short_description: Domoticz is a free, open-source, self-hosted home automation system written in C++ that monitors and controls smart home devices like lights, sensors, and switches.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Domoticz is a free, open-source home automation system for Linux, Windows, and Raspberry Pi. See 2026 features, supported devices, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Domoticz is a free and open-source home automation platform that lets users monitor, configure, and automate smart home devices such as lights, switches, thermostats, and a wide range of sensors covering temperature, humidity, rain, wind, UV, gas, water, and electricity usage. Unlike cloud-dependent smart home ecosystems, Domoticz is entirely self-hosted: it runs on hardware the user controls, keeps all data local, and requires no cloud account or recurring subscription to function.

The project traces its origin to a Dutch developer who built the software to support the Netherlands' P1 Smart Meter standard and devices connected through the RFXCOM RFXtrx433 transceiver. It was released publicly as open source around Christmas 2012 and has been maintained continuously since, evolving into a broader home automation controller that now supports more than 150 hardware devices and protocols, including Z-Wave, Zigbee, MQTT, and Philips Hue. Because Domoticz is a community-driven open-source project rather than a company, there is no headquarters, ownership structure, or venture funding behind it; development happens on GitHub with contributions from volunteers, and localization is crowdsourced through community-maintained translation files covering languages including Dutch, French, German, and Portuguese in addition to English.

Technically, Domoticz is written in C++, which the project cites as the reason it can run comfortably on constrained hardware - reportedly using under 50MB of RAM, far less than Python- or Java-based home automation platforms. It can run on anything from a Raspberry Pi Zero to a full Linux or Windows server, and also ships an official Android client application for mobile access alongside its built-in, responsive HTML5 web interface that adapts automatically to desktop and mobile browsers. Automation logic can be written using several scripting options: the Lua-based dzVents engine (the most commonly recommended approach for new event scripts), a visual Blockly editor for users who prefer not to write code, or Python for more advanced integrations. A JSON-based API is also exposed, letting third-party dashboards, scripts, and integrations query and control the system programmatically.

Domoticz has no pricing structure in the commercial sense: the software itself, its source code, and its official mobile app are all free, distributed under an open-source license, and there are no tiered plans, subscriptions, or paywalled features. The only costs a user incurs are for the physical hardware they choose to run it on (a Raspberry Pi, NAS, old PC, or similar) and for any smart home devices and radio transceivers (Z-Wave, Zigbee, RFXCOM, etc.) they want to integrate.

The platform is aimed squarely at technically inclined hobbyists, DIY smart home builders, and privacy-conscious users who want full local control over their automation rather than relying on a vendor's cloud service. It is particularly well suited to users with older or unusual hardware, resource-constrained devices, or a strong preference for avoiding cloud lock-in and monthly fees. Domoticz's most direct competitor is Home Assistant, a Python-based open-source platform that has grown into the most widely used option in this category, with a far larger integration library, community, and update cadence; Domoticz differentiates itself with a dramatically lighter resource footprint, strong support for legacy protocols like 433MHz RF, and long-term configuration stability that some users prefer over Home Assistant's faster-moving ecosystem. Other adjacent competitors include openHAB and commercial hub-based systems like Homey.

Notable facts about Domoticz include its origin as a project built to support Dutch smart meter hardware, its C++ implementation that keeps it usable on extremely low-power devices such as a Raspberry Pi Zero, its roughly 3,500 GitHub stars and active community forum, and its position as one of the longer-running self-hosted home automation projects still under active development, having been maintained continuously since 2012.

Domoticz's device support extends well beyond its original RFXCOM and P1 smart-meter roots. Over the years the project has added native or plugin-based support for Z-Wave controllers, Zigbee coordinators (including Conbee/deCONZ-based setups), MQTT brokers for integrating with a huge range of DIY and commercial sensors, Philips Hue bridges, KNX systems, P1 Dutch and Belgian smart meters, weather station hardware, and many 433MHz RF devices from brands like Chacon, Kaku, and Oregon Scientific. This breadth means Domoticz is frequently chosen by users migrating old or mixed-vendor smart home setups accumulated over many years, where a newer platform's integration list might not reach back far enough to cover legacy hardware. A companion project called Dashticz, built by community members, provides an alternative, more modern-looking dashboard front end that talks to a Domoticz backend over its JSON API, illustrating how the ecosystem has grown organically around the core server software.

Governance of the project happens transparently on GitHub, where the core domoticz/domoticz repository accepts community pull requests, and translation of the interface is managed through the Transifex platform, allowing volunteer translators to contribute and update language files independently of core code releases. Development activity has slowed somewhat compared to its peak years as more of the DIY home automation community has migrated toward Home Assistant, but Domoticz continues to receive periodic beta and stable releases, bug fixes, and new device integrations, and its forum remains an active resource for troubleshooting and hardware compatibility questions. For users evaluating the space in 2026, the realistic decision is less 'Domoticz versus Home Assistant' in the abstract and more a question of whether raw resource efficiency and legacy protocol support (Domoticz's strengths) matter more than ecosystem size and newer-standard support such as Matter and Thread (Home Assistant's strengths) for a given hardware setup.

Installation methods for Domoticz reflect its emphasis on running anywhere: pre-built binary packages are available for Debian and Ubuntu-based Linux distributions, a Windows installer is provided for users who prefer running it on a spare PC, and Docker images let users deploy it inside containerized home server setups such as those built on Unraid, Synology, or QNAP network-attached storage devices. Because it has no mandatory cloud dependency, a Domoticz installation continues functioning even during an internet outage, a property some users specifically value for critical automations like heating control or security-adjacent sensors that should not depend on an external connection to keep working.

It monitors and controls devices such as lights, switches, thermostats, and a wide range of sensors, and supports more than 150 hardware protocols including Z-Wave, Zigbee, MQTT, and Philips Hue.

Originally released in 2012 by a Dutch developer to support the Netherlands' P1 Smart Meter and RFXCOM RF hardware, Domoticz has grown into a community-maintained open-source project with contributors on GitHub and crowdsourced translations into languages beyond English.

faqs.question: Is Domoticz free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Domoticz is fully free and open source, with no subscriptions, license fees, or paid tiers.

faqs.question: What platforms does Domoticz run on?
faqs.answer: Domoticz runs on Linux, Windows, and Raspberry Pi, and it can operate on hardware as small as a Raspberry Pi Zero thanks to its lightweight C++ codebase.

faqs.question: Does Domoticz require an internet connection?
faqs.answer: No. Domoticz runs and automates entirely on local hardware; an internet connection is only needed for optional integrations with external cloud services.

faqs.question: How is Domoticz different from Home Assistant?
faqs.answer: Domoticz is significantly lighter on system resources and has strong legacy protocol support, while Home Assistant has a much larger integration library, community, and faster release cadence.

faqs.question: Can I automate Domoticz without writing code?
faqs.answer: Yes. Domoticz includes a visual Blockly editor for building automations without code, in addition to Lua-based dzVents and Python scripting for more advanced use cases.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app for Domoticz?
faqs.answer: Yes, Domoticz has an official Android client application, and its web interface is also mobile-responsive for use in any modern browser.

enrichment.short_description: Doodle is an online scheduling platform that helps people coordinate meeting times through group polls, one-on-one booking, and public booking pages that sync with popular calendars.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Doodle helps teams schedule meetings with polls, 1:1 booking, and booking pages. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and how it compares to Calendly.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Doodle is an online scheduling and calendar coordination platform best known for its meeting polls, which let an organizer propose several possible dates and times and have invitees vote on the ones that work for them. Over time the product has expanded well beyond polls into a broader scheduling suite that also includes Doodle 1:1 (quick one-on-one meeting booking) and Booking Page (a public, Calendly-style page where others can book available time directly on an organizer's calendar), positioning Doodle as a general-purpose scheduling tool rather than a single-feature poll app.

Doodle was founded in 2007 in Zurich, Switzerland, by two ETH Zurich students, Michael Naf and Paul Sevinc, who built the original polling tool to solve their own difficulty finding meeting times among groups. The product grew quickly on the strength of its simplicity and became one of the most widely recognized names in online scheduling, particularly in Europe. Swiss media company TX Group took an early minority stake in Doodle in 2011 and increased its ownership to 100 percent by 2014, later formalizing full acquisition in 2022. Doodle is headquartered in Zurich with additional offices in Berlin, Belgrade, Atlanta, and New York, and operates with a lean team of roughly 120 employees spread across those locations.

Functionally, Doodle integrates with major calendar systems including Google Calendar, Outlook, Office 365, and iCal, so availability shown to invitees reflects a user's real, up-to-date calendar rather than manually maintained data. It natively integrates with popular video conferencing tools such as Google Meet, Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Webex, automatically attaching a meeting link once a time is confirmed. Doodle also supports Stripe-based payment collection on booking pages, which is useful for consultants and service providers who charge for booked time. For workflows outside these native integrations, Doodle relies on Zapier rather than offering a large first-party integration marketplace, which is a notable difference from competitors like Calendly.

Doodle's pricing follows a freemium model. A free plan supports basic polling and one booking page with limited functionality. The paid Pro plan costs $6.95 per user per month when billed annually, aimed at individual professionals who need unlimited polls, booking pages, and calendar integrations without team-management features. The Team plan costs $8.95 per user per month (also billed annually) and supports up to five users, adding collaborative scheduling features such as shared booking pages and team availability views. Organizations with larger or more complex scheduling needs can contact Doodle for a custom-quoted Enterprise plan that adds SSO, advanced admin controls, and dedicated support.

Doodle's core audience spans individual professionals, small teams, and larger organizations that need to coordinate meetings across many participants and time zones - a use case where its original polling format still has a distinct advantage over link-based scheduling tools. Common users include recruiters coordinating interview panels, sales and customer success teams booking calls, educators scheduling office hours, and event organizers finding a date that works for a large group. Its most direct competitor is Calendly, which focuses primarily on single-use, link-based booking and offers a broader third-party integration ecosystem, while Doodle differentiates itself through its original group-polling strength and native video-conferencing and payment integrations. Other adjacent alternatives include Microsoft Bookings, When2meet, and Google Calendar's built-in appointment scheduling features.

Notable facts about Doodle include its origin as a student side project at ETH Zurich that became one of the best-known scheduling brands globally, its full ownership by Swiss media group TX Group since 2022, and its continued emphasis on group availability polling as a differentiator even as the broader scheduling software category has shifted toward single-link, Calendly-style booking pages.

Doodle's growth trajectory illustrates a common pattern for early web-era products: it became a household name and a verb of sorts ('just doodle it') among European professionals and academics in the late 2000s and early 2010s, at a time when finding a shared meeting time by email thread was the norm. As the scheduling software category matured and competitors like Calendly, Cal.com, and Microsoft Bookings introduced link-based, single-invitee booking as the default paradigm, Doodle broadened its own product to match, adding Doodle 1:1 and Booking Page rather than remaining a pure polling tool. This evolution reflects the company's recognition that group polling, while still valuable, is only one segment of the broader meeting-scheduling workflow that professionals need covered.

Doodle's ownership under TX Group, the publisher behind major Swiss media brands, has provided the company with financial stability uncommon for a scheduling SaaS company of its size, allowing it to prioritize product steadiness over the aggressive fundraising-and-growth cycles typical of venture-backed competitors. Independent user reviews on sites like G2 and Capterra generally rate Doodle highly for simplicity and reliability, with the most common critique being that its integration ecosystem, while covering the essentials (major calendars and video tools), is narrower than Calendly's extensive native app marketplace, pushing more advanced automation needs toward Zapier-based workarounds. For organizations whose primary pain point is coordinating meetings across many stakeholders and time zones rather than exposing a single public booking link, Doodle's polling heritage still gives it a practical edge over booking-link-first competitors.

Doodle also offers apps for iOS and Android that mirror the core web experience, letting organizers create polls, respond to invitations, and manage bookings from a phone, which matters for a scheduling tool whose value depends on invitees being able to respond quickly regardless of device. The company publishes its own comparison pages against Calendly and other competitors directly on doodle.com, a common practice among scheduling SaaS vendors competing for the same searches, and independent buyer guides generally agree that the choice between Doodle and its closest rivals comes down to whether an organization's primary workflow is many-to-many group coordination (Doodle's strength) or one-to-many public booking (where Calendly and similar tools are typically preferred).

Founded in 2007 by ETH Zurich students Michael Naf and Paul Sevinc, Doodle is headquartered in Zurich, Switzerland and has been fully owned by Swiss media company TX Group since 2022, operating with offices in Zurich, Berlin, Belgrade, Atlanta, and New York.

faqs.answer: Doodle's Pro plan costs $6.95 per user per month billed annually, and the Team plan (up to five users) costs $8.95 per user per month billed annually. Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted.

faqs.question: Who founded Doodle?
faqs.answer: Doodle was founded in 2007 by ETH Zurich students Michael Naf and Paul Sevinc, and it is now fully owned by the Swiss media company TX Group.

faqs.question: What is Doodle used for?
faqs.answer: Doodle is used to coordinate meeting times across groups through polls, book one-on-one meetings, and let others book open time on a calendar through a public booking page.

faqs.question: How is Doodle different from Calendly?
faqs.answer: Doodle is best known for its group-polling functionality for coordinating many participants, while Calendly focuses primarily on fast, single-use, link-based individual bookings with a larger native integration ecosystem.

faqs.question: Does Doodle integrate with video conferencing tools?
faqs.answer: Yes. Doodle natively integrates with Google Meet, Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Webex, automatically attaching a meeting link once a time is confirmed.

enrichment.short_description: Doppler is a developer-first secrets management platform that centralizes, syncs, and rotates API keys, environment variables, and credentials across applications and environments.
enrichment.seo_title: Doppler Review 2026: Secrets Management Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Doppler centralizes secrets management for dev teams. See 2026 pricing (Developer, Team, Enterprise), key features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Doppler is a secrets management platform designed to eliminate hardcoded API keys, database credentials, and other sensitive configuration values scattered across a company's codebase, CI pipelines, and infrastructure. Instead of storing secrets in .env files, source control, or scattered dashboard settings, teams centralize them in Doppler and then sync or fetch them programmatically into every environment that needs them, with Doppler acting as the single source of truth for configuration across development, staging, and production.

The company was founded in 2018 by Brian Vallelunga, Jack Legg, and Cesar D'Onofrio, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California (some records also reference offices in nearby California cities). Doppler went through Y Combinator and has raised approximately $28.9 million in total funding across multiple rounds from investors including Google Ventures, Sequoia Capital, Y Combinator, and CRV, notably including a $20 million Series A round announced in 2022. As of recent company disclosures, Doppler serves more than 31,500 customers and its platform handles billions of secret fetches per month, reflecting significant adoption among engineering teams that treat secrets management as core infrastructure rather than an afterthought.

Technically, Doppler works by letting teams define 'configs' - named collections of secrets scoped to a project and environment (for example, a project's development, staging, and production configs) - which can inherit from one another to avoid duplicating shared values. Applications and infrastructure retrieve secrets through the Doppler CLI, SDKs, a REST API, or native integrations with platforms like Kubernetes, Docker, Vercel, AWS, GitHub Actions, and Terraform, so a secret rotated once in Doppler propagates everywhere it is referenced without manual redeployment of every consumer. The platform also supports service tokens and service accounts for machine-to-machine access, change requests and approval workflows for sensitive updates, role-based access control, and detailed activity logging so security and platform teams can audit who accessed or changed which secrets and when. More recent product positioning emphasizes that non-human identities and AI agents can access secrets under the same governance model without incurring additional per-seat costs, a response to the growing number of automated and AI-driven systems that need credentialed access to infrastructure.

Doppler's pricing is structured around three tiers with transparent, publicly listed pricing rather than the opaque quote-based model used by some competitors. The Developer plan is free for up to three users and then $8 per additional user per month, and includes the Doppler CLI, service tokens, email alerts, three-day activity log retention, secrets referencing, API and webhook access, and support for five config syncs. The Team plan, Doppler's most popular tier, costs $21 per user per month (with a 14-day free trial) and adds change requests, SAML single sign-on, role-based access controls, 90-day activity logs, automatic secret rotation, service accounts, config inheritance, priority support, and 100 config syncs. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and scales with team size, adding on-premise or private cloud deployment options, custom permissions and user groups, enterprise SCIM provisioning, Terraform-managed infrastructure, proxied secret rotation, enterprise key management, dynamic secrets, log forwarding to external SIEM tools, unlimited config syncs, a 99.95% uptime SLO, and a dedicated account manager.

Doppler's target users are software engineering and DevOps/platform teams at startups through large enterprises that need a centralized, auditable way to manage secrets across many services, environments, and cloud providers, without building and maintaining that infrastructure themselves. It is particularly popular with teams building modern, cloud-native, multi-environment applications who want a developer-friendly CLI and SDK experience rather than a heavyweight enterprise security console. Its most direct competitors are HashiCorp Vault, a highly configurable and self-hostable secrets platform favored by large enterprises with dedicated platform engineering teams and complex dynamic-secrets requirements; AWS Secrets Manager and Azure Key Vault, cloud-native managed services best suited to teams already committed to a single cloud provider; and newer developer-focused entrants like Infisical. Doppler differentiates itself by prioritizing developer experience and fast time-to-production over the deepest possible feature set, offering a managed SaaS product (with an on-premise Enterprise option) rather than requiring teams to operate their own Vault cluster, and by explicitly not charging extra for non-human and AI agent access to secrets.

Notable facts about Doppler include its Y Combinator and Sequoia-backed funding history, its claimed scale of over 6.5 billion secret fetches processed monthly, its transparent public pricing (unusual in the security-tooling space, where quote-based enterprise pricing is common), and its recent strategic emphasis on serving AI agents and other non-human identities as first-class consumers of secrets alongside human developers.

Doppler's founding story traces back to its co-founders' own frustration managing scattered environment variables and API keys across growing engineering teams, a pain point common enough that it has spawned an entire category of secrets-management startups over the past decade. The company's early Y Combinator batch positioning centered on making secrets management something a small team could adopt in an afternoon rather than a multi-week platform-engineering project, and that developer-experience-first philosophy has remained central to its product decisions even as it has added enterprise features like SSO, SCIM, and dynamic secrets to compete for larger accounts. Doppler reports its dashboard and CLI are used by engineering teams ranging from early-stage startups to publicly traded technology companies, and its integration list has grown to cover most major cloud providers, container orchestration platforms, CI/CD systems, and infrastructure-as-code tools that a modern engineering team is likely to already be running.

The company's more recent product messaging around AI agents reflects a broader shift in the secrets-management category: as more automated systems, coding agents, and AI-driven pipelines require their own credentialed access to APIs and infrastructure, secrets platforms that charge strictly per human seat gain an advantage over those that would otherwise need to license every bot or automation account as a full user. Doppler has leaned into this positioning explicitly, marketing itself as secrets management 'for humans and AI agents' on its own homepage, treating non-human identities as a governed but not separately monetized class of consumer. This makes Doppler a notable case study in how developer-tooling companies are adjusting pricing models for an era in which a growing share of infrastructure access is initiated by software rather than people.

faqs.answer: Doppler is a managed, developer-friendly SaaS product optimized for fast setup and ease of use, while HashiCorp Vault is a highly configurable, self-hostable platform with deeper dynamic-secrets capabilities aimed at large enterprises with dedicated platform engineering teams.

enrichment.short_description: Dorik is an AI-powered, no-code website builder that lets users create, host, and publish professional websites using templates, drag-and-drop blocks, and a built-in CMS.
enrichment.seo_title: Dorik Review 2026: No-Code Website Builder Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dorik is an AI no-code website builder with 110+ templates and a built-in CMS. See 2026 pricing plans, key features, pros, cons, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dorik is an AI-assisted, no-code website builder that lets individuals and small businesses design, host, and publish professional websites without writing code. Users start from one of more than 110 ready-made templates or generate a starting layout with AI, then customize the page using more than 250 drag-and-drop UI blocks, a visual editor, and a built-in content management system for handling blog posts and dynamic content.

Dorik was founded in 2019 by Mizanur Rahman and Anwar Hossain as a side project before officially launching publicly in August 2020, where it reached the number-two spot on Product Hunt's Product of the Day. The company is notable for being based in Sylhet, Bangladesh, rather than a traditional startup hub, and has grown into a globally used SaaS product serving customers well beyond its home market; public reporting on the company's growth has cited annual recurring revenue in the range of roughly $11 million, reflecting meaningful scale for a bootstrapped, non-Silicon-Valley team. The founders have spoken publicly about building Dorik specifically to make professional website creation accessible to people without technical or design backgrounds, a mission reflected in the product's heavy use of templates and pre-built blocks rather than a blank-canvas page builder.

Technically, Dorik is a fully hosted, browser-based website builder: there is no software to install, and published sites run on Dorik's own hosting infrastructure with unlimited bandwidth and storage included even on lower tiers. The editor supports custom domains, a white-label dashboard option for agencies reselling websites to their own clients, and code export so more advanced users can extract the underlying HTML/CSS if they need to host elsewhere or hand off a project. The built-in CMS supports blog posts and other structured content types, and Dorik's AI tools can generate page copy, layouts, and images to speed up the initial build process, positioning the product between fully manual no-code builders like Webflow and heavily automated AI site generators.

Dorik's pricing follows a freemium model with several ways to pay. The Free plan includes one custom domain, up to 25 pages, up to 200 blog posts, and unlimited storage and bandwidth, with Dorik branding included. Paid plans are available on monthly, annual, biennial (2-year), and one-time lifetime billing, with meaningful discounts for longer commitments. On monthly billing, the Personal plan costs $29/month, the Business plan costs $59/month, and the Agency plan costs $99/month; billed annually, those same plans work out to $249/year, $498/year, and $948/year respectively, and 2-year billing lowers the effective monthly cost further. The Personal plan supports one custom domain, 25 pages, and 200 blog posts; the Business plan raises that to three custom domains, unlimited pages, up to 10 collaborators, and unlimited blog posts; and the Agency plan removes custom domain, page, collaborator, and blog post limits entirely, aimed at agencies managing many client sites. Dorik has also offered one-time lifetime-access pricing on Personal and Business tiers for customers who prefer to avoid recurring billing, and an Agency tier is available on request for custom needs. All paid plans include AI website generation, a white-label dashboard, and code export.

Dorik's target users are solo entrepreneurs, freelancers, small businesses, and digital agencies who want to launch a professional-looking website quickly without hiring a developer or learning a complex design tool. Agencies in particular are drawn to the white-label dashboard and unlimited-site Agency plan, which let them build and manage many client sites under their own brand from a single Dorik account. Its main competitors include Webflow, which offers deeper design control and a steeper learning curve aimed at professional designers; Wix and Squarespace, more consumer-oriented builders with larger template ecosystems and app marketplaces; and Framer, a newer AI-and-design-forward competitor. Dorik differentiates itself primarily on price and simplicity relative to Webflow, and on offering a genuinely usable free plan and code export option that many competitors restrict or omit.

Notable facts about Dorik include its origin as a bootstrapped side project built outside a major tech hub, its recognition as a Product Hunt top product at launch, and its emphasis on serving agencies through white-labeling, which distinguishes it from website builders aimed primarily at individual end users.

Dorik's founders have described the company's origin as a direct response to their own experience trying to build websites for local businesses in Bangladesh, where existing no-code tools were often expensive, slow for local connection speeds, or not well suited to the needs of small, non-technical business owners. That grounding in a genuinely underserved market segment has been credited by outside observers and startup commentators as a key reason Dorik found early traction: rather than competing head-on with Webflow for professional designers, it targeted small business owners and freelancers who needed something simpler and cheaper, then expanded upward toward the agency segment once the core product matured. Public interviews with co-founder Mizanur Rahman describe the company scaling to seven figures in annual recurring revenue while remaining a lean, largely bootstrapped team, a notable achievement for a SaaS company built and operated from Sylhet rather than a conventional startup hub in North America or Western Europe.

Product-wise, Dorik has continued to invest in its AI capabilities beyond basic page generation, adding AI-assisted image generation, copywriting suggestions, and layout recommendations intended to compress the time between signing up and publishing a usable site. The company markets this combination of AI generation plus a traditional block-based editor as a middle path between fully automated 'one-click AI website' generators, which often produce generic results that are hard to customize, and fully manual builders like Webflow, which offer maximum control but demand a steeper learning investment. This positioning has made Dorik a recurring recommendation in roundup articles targeting small business owners, solo consultants, and agencies looking for an affordable Webflow alternative rather than enterprise marketing teams, who are more likely to evaluate higher-end platforms with deeper marketing-automation integrations.

enrichment.short_description: Dotclear is a free, open-source blog publishing and content management system built in PHP, known for its simplicity, speed, and strong following among French-speaking bloggers.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dotclear is a free, open-source PHP blogging platform with themes, plugins, and multi-blog support. See 2026 features, pricing, and how it compares to WordPress.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dotclear is a free, open-source content management system built specifically for blogging, distributed for self-hosting on any server capable of running PHP. It lets a single installation host and manage multiple independent blogs, supports writing entries in either wiki syntax or XHTML, allows pages that sit outside the normal chronological entry flow, and includes native podcast publishing support for creators who want to distribute audio content alongside written posts.

The project was originally developed by French programmer Olivier Meunier starting in 2002 and was first published as open-source software in 2003, making it one of the older blogging platforms still in active development today, predating WordPress by roughly a year. Over more than two decades, Dotclear has attracted a dedicated core team of volunteer developers who continue to release regular updates; version 2.33, released in February 2025, continued the project's ongoing modernization effort, including a shift from jQuery toward vanilla JavaScript in the admin interface and improvements to media management and public session handling. As with many long-running open-source projects, Dotclear is not owned or operated by a company - it has no headquarters, employee count, or commercial ownership structure, and is instead maintained by its community of contributors.

Technically, Dotclear is written in PHP and supports multiple database backends, including MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, and SQLite, giving self-hosters flexibility depending on their existing server environment. The platform's functionality can be extended through a large library of community-built plugins and widgets, and its appearance can be customized through user-contributed themes, both distributed primarily via the DotAddict.org community site. Licensing has evolved over the project's history: Dotclear was originally released under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), and in March 2024 the project moved to the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3), a stronger copyleft license that also covers software offered as a network service.

Because Dotclear is fully open source and self-hosted, there is no commercial pricing structure, no subscription tiers, and no paid version of the core software; the only costs a user incurs are for the web hosting and domain they choose to run it on. Some hosting providers offer one-click Dotclear installers (similar to WordPress hosting), but this reflects hosting convenience rather than any official paid tier from the Dotclear project itself.

Dotclear's user base and cultural footprint are heavily concentrated in French-speaking countries; research indicates over 80 percent of identifiable Dotclear installations are based in France, and it is used by notable French institutions and media-adjacent blogging platforms, including outlets like Gandi Blogs and the French Navy's (Marine nationale) blogging presence. Globally, Dotclear holds a very small share of the overall content management system and blogging platform market - under 0.01 percent of tracked CMS installations - reflecting its status as a niche, regionally concentrated tool rather than a mainstream, globally dominant platform. Its closest competitors in the self-hosted blogging space are WordPress, by far the dominant open-source CMS worldwide, and smaller alternatives like Ghost and Grav; among hosted blogging services, it also competes indirectly with Tumblr, Medium, and Google Blogger, though those are closed, non-self-hosted products with a fundamentally different value proposition. Dotclear differentiates itself from WordPress primarily through a simpler, more focused feature set aimed squarely at blogging rather than general-purpose website building, along with a reputation for being lightweight and fast, and for maintaining a smaller attack surface and plugin ecosystem than WordPress's much larger, more heterogeneous plugin marketplace.

Notable facts about Dotclear include its origin as a solo project by Olivier Meunier in 2002, its 2024 relicensing to AGPLv3, its strong and disproportionate popularity in France relative to the rest of the world, and its status as one of the longest continuously maintained open-source blogging platforms, having outlasted many contemporaries from the mid-2000s blogging software era.

Dotclear's administration interface is organized around a familiar blogging workflow: an entries list for managing posts and their publication status, a media manager for uploading and organizing images and files, a comments and trackback moderation queue, and a blog-configuration section for managing themes, plugins, and multi-blog settings from one place. Because the software supports running many independently configured blogs from a single codebase and database, it has historically appealed to organizations, agencies, and prolific individual writers who want to manage several distinct publications without maintaining separate installations, a use case that some competitors handle only through more complex multisite configurations. The DotAddict.org community site functions as the de facto plugin and theme marketplace for the ecosystem, hosting hundreds of community-contributed extensions covering everything from SEO helpers and social sharing widgets to more specialized tools like series/tag management and custom widget blocks for sidebars.

The project's decision to move to AGPLv3 in 2024 is notable within the open-source CMS world because that license explicitly closes the 'SaaS loophole' present in plain GPL, meaning that anyone who modifies Dotclear and offers it as a network service (for example, a hosted blogging platform built on a modified Dotclear core) is also obligated to release their modified source code, not just users who receive the software directly. This is a stronger copyleft stance than WordPress's GPLv2 licensing and reflects the Dotclear team's continued commitment to keeping the software and any derivatives fully open, even as the broader web publishing landscape has consolidated around a small number of dominant, commercially backed platforms.

Because Dotclear has no company or paid support tier of its own, users who want managed hosting or migration assistance typically rely on general-purpose PHP web hosts that support one-click installers, or on independent consultants within the French-speaking Dotclear community who offer setup and customization services informally. This differs from WordPress's ecosystem, where an enormous number of specialized managed-hosting companies (such as WP Engine and Kinsta) and agencies exist specifically to support that one platform; Dotclear's smaller scale means support is more community-driven and less commercially formalized, which is a tradeoff self-hosters should weigh alongside the platform's simplicity and lighter resource footprint.

faqs.question: What license does Dotclear use?
faqs.answer: Dotclear was originally released under GPLv2 and moved to the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPLv3) in March 2024.

faqs.question: How does Dotclear compare to WordPress?
faqs.answer: Dotclear is smaller and more narrowly focused on blogging, with a lighter footprint and smaller plugin ecosystem than WordPress, which dominates the broader CMS and website-building market.

faqs.question: What programming language is Dotclear written in?
faqs.answer: Dotclear is written in PHP and supports MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, and SQLite databases.

faqs.question: Where is Dotclear most popular?
faqs.answer: Dotclear's usage is heavily concentrated in France and other French-speaking regions, where it is used by several notable blogging platforms and institutions.

enrichment.short_description: Dovecot is a widely deployed, security-focused open-source IMAP and POP3 email server for Unix-like systems, available as a free Community Edition or a commercially supported Pro Edition.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovecot is a secure, open-source IMAP/POP3 mail server. See 2026 features, the free Community Edition vs. commercial Pro Edition, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dovecot is an open-source mail server implementing the IMAP and POP3 protocols for Unix-like operating systems, built with security, standards compliance, and performance as its founding priorities. Rather than functioning as a complete email solution on its own, Dovecot handles the mailbox storage and retrieval side of email infrastructure - the piece that lets a mail client fetch and organize a user's messages - and is typically deployed alongside a separate mail transfer agent such as Postfix or OpenSMTPD, which handles sending and routing mail between servers.

Dovecot was originated by Finnish developer Timo Sirainen, who released the first version in July 2002. Over the following two decades it grew into one of the most widely deployed mail server implementations in the world, commonly cited as the most-used open-source IMAP server, running on a large share of the internet's self-hosted and hosting-provider mail infrastructure. In 2017, the Mozilla Open Source Support program funded the first public third-party security audit of the Dovecot codebase, a milestone that reinforced its reputation as a security-conscious project in an area of software - email storage - where vulnerabilities can expose highly sensitive personal and business communications. The project's commercial and enterprise direction is now stewarded by Open-Xchange, a German-headquartered email and collaboration software company, which develops and sells Dovecot Pro as a commercial product built on top of the open-source core.

Technically, Dovecot implements the IMAP (RFC 3501) and POP3 (RFC 1939) protocols along with LMTP (RFC 2033) for local mail delivery and ManageSieve (RFC 5228) for remote management of Sieve mail-filtering scripts, letting users define server-side rules for sorting, forwarding, or discarding incoming mail. It supports a range of authentication mechanisms, including CRAM-MD5 and DIGEST-MD5, and version 2.3 added a built-in message submission agent along with Lua scripting support for authentication logic. For searching large mailboxes quickly, Dovecot offers full-text search (FTS) plugins; the long-standing option integrates with the Apache Solr search platform, while newer Dovecot 2.4+ releases introduced the fts_flatcurve plugin, which provides built-in full-text search using the Xapian library without requiring an external search server. Dovecot is also known for self-optimizing mailbox indexes and self-healing mechanisms designed to recover gracefully from data corruption, along with straightforward migration tooling for moving mailboxes over from other mail server software.

Dovecot's open-source Community Edition (CE) is completely free to download, use, and modify, released primarily under LGPLv2.1 and MIT-style licensing, and is designed for use on a single server - the typical setup for small hosting providers, ISPs, and self-hosters running their own mail infrastructure. For organizations operating at larger scale, Open-Xchange sells Dovecot Pro Edition, which layers proprietary enhancements onto the open-source core, including a cloud-native, stateless clustering architecture (referred to as Palomar) built for high availability and horizontal scalability across many servers, along with dedicated vendor support. Dovecot Pro pricing is not published as a fixed list price; Open-Xchange prices it based on the number of mailboxes under management, with per-mailbox costs decreasing at higher volumes, and offers multiple support tiers; one reference point from a Microsoft Azure-hosted managed offering priced Dovecot-based managed email at around $1 per mailbox, though that reflects a specific managed-cloud packaging rather than Dovecot Pro's standard licensing.

Dovecot's target users span the full range from individual hobbyists self-hosting personal email on a single Linux server, through small and mid-sized web hosting companies bundling mail hosting for their customers, up to large internet service providers and enterprises running Dovecot Pro at carrier scale to serve millions of mailboxes. Its most direct competitors in the open-source IMAP server space include Cyrus IMAP and the now largely legacy Courier-IMAP, while at the managed and enterprise end it competes indirectly with hosted and on-premise alternatives like Microsoft Exchange. Dovecot differentiates itself primarily through its reputation for security, standards compliance, and efficient performance, plus the option to scale from a free single-server deployment up to a fully supported, horizontally scalable commercial product without switching software.

Notable facts about Dovecot include its 2002 origin as a project by Timo Sirainen, its status as likely the most widely deployed open-source IMAP server on the internet, the 2017 Mozilla-funded security audit of its codebase, and its dual-track development model in which a free, community-maintained core coexists with a commercially supported Pro Edition sold by Open-Xchange for large-scale, carrier-grade deployments.

Dovecot's design philosophy from its earliest days emphasized security-by-default configuration choices, aiming to make it hard for an administrator to accidentally deploy an insecure mail server, in contrast to some older mail server software that shipped with more permissive default settings. This emphasis, combined with a relatively small and auditable core codebase compared to some legacy Unix mail systems, is a large part of why Dovecot displaced older IMAP servers like UW-IMAP and Courier-IMAP as the default choice bundled with most major Linux distributions and hosting control panels, including cPanel, Plesk, and most self-hosted mail stack tutorials built around Postfix. Because Dovecot only handles the IMAP/POP3 and local-delivery side of the stack, it is almost always documented and deployed alongside Postfix for outbound SMTP relay, and the Postfix-plus-Dovecot combination has effectively become the default reference architecture for self-hosted email on Linux over the past fifteen years.

Open-Xchange's stewardship of the commercial side of the project since acquiring the trademark and hiring much of the core team means that Dovecot today operates on a model similar to other successful open-core infrastructure projects: the Community Edition remains genuinely free and functionally complete for single-server use, while revenue is generated from Pro Edition customers who need clustering, high availability, and vendor-backed support at a scale where self-managing a bespoke multi-server Dovecot cluster would be operationally risky. This structure has let Dovecot avoid the fate of some open-source infrastructure projects that either stagnate without commercial backing or restrict their free tier so heavily that it stops being genuinely useful; Dovecot's free Community Edition continues to receive regular security patches and feature updates even though it is not the commercially monetized version of the product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dovecot is a security-focused, open-source mail server implementing the IMAP and POP3 protocols for Unix-like systems. It handles mailbox storage and retrieval, and is typically paired with a separate mail transfer agent like Postfix for sending and routing mail.

Originated by Finnish developer Timo Sirainen with a first release in July 2002, Dovecot grew into one of the most widely deployed open-source IMAP servers in the world, with its commercial direction now stewarded by Open-Xchange through the Dovecot Pro product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Dovecot implements IMAP, POP3, LMTP for local delivery, and ManageSieve for remote mail-filtering rule management, with support for authentication mechanisms like CRAM-MD5 and DIGEST-MD5.

Full-text search is available through Solr integration or the newer built-in fts_flatcurve plugin (Dovecot 2.4+), and the platform is known for self-optimizing indexes and self-healing recovery from data corruption.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is completely free and open source, designed for single-server deployments. Dovecot Pro, sold by Open-Xchange for larger-scale and enterprise deployments, is priced per mailbox with volume-based discounts and is custom-quoted rather than listed publicly.

features.description: Implements RFC-standard IMAP and POP3 for reliable, standards-compliant mailbox access across mail clients.

features.description: Supports the Local Mail Transfer Protocol for efficient handoff of mail from an MTA into user mailboxes.

features.description: Lets users define and remotely manage server-side rules for sorting, forwarding, or discarding mail.

features.title: Self-optimizing indexes
features.description: Automatically maintains efficient mailbox indexes to keep large mailboxes fast to search and browse.

features.title: Self-healing data integrity
features.description: Includes mechanisms to detect and recover from mailbox data corruption without manual intervention.

features.title: Full-text search plugins
features.description: Supports Apache Solr integration or the newer built-in Xapian-based fts_flatcurve plugin for fast mailbox search.

features.title: Flexible authentication
features.description: Supports multiple authentication schemes including CRAM-MD5, DIGEST-MD5, and Lua-scripted authentication logic.

features.title: Dovecot Pro clustering
features.description: The commercial Pro Edition adds a cloud-native, stateless clustering architecture for high availability at carrier scale.

pros: Free, mature, and extremely widely deployed open-source mail server with a strong security track record.
Publicly audited codebase, including a 2017 Mozilla-funded third-party security review.
Scales from a single free self-hosted server up to a fully supported commercial product without changing software.
Standards-compliant implementation with broad mail client compatibility.
cons: Requires a separate mail transfer agent (such as Postfix) to form a complete email system, adding setup complexity.
Dovecot Pro pricing is not publicly listed and requires contacting Open-Xchange for a quote.
Community Edition is designed for single-server use, so scaling requires either manual clustering or upgrading to Pro.
Administration is command-line and configuration-file driven, with a learning curve for those unfamiliar with Unix mail systems.

faqs.question: Is Dovecot free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Dovecot Community Edition is free and open source. Open-Xchange also sells a commercially supported Pro Edition priced per mailbox for larger-scale deployments.

faqs.question: Who created Dovecot?
faqs.answer: Dovecot was created by Finnish developer Timo Sirainen, who released the first version in July 2002.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Dovecot and Postfix?
faqs.answer: Dovecot handles IMAP/POP3 mailbox storage and retrieval for end users, while Postfix is a mail transfer agent that sends and routes mail between servers; the two are typically deployed together to form a complete email system.

faqs.question: How much does Dovecot Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Dovecot Pro pricing is not published publicly; Open-Xchange prices it based on the number of mailboxes under management, with lower per-mailbox costs at higher volumes, and requires a custom quote.

faqs.question: Has Dovecot been security audited?
faqs.answer: Yes. In 2017, the Mozilla Open Source Support program funded the first public third-party security audit of the Dovecot codebase.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Dovecot run on?
faqs.answer: Dovecot runs on Unix-like operating systems, including Linux and BSD variants.

enrichment.short_description: Dovel is a free, open-source, lightweight SMTP server written in Go that lets developers self-host email sending and receiving for one or more domains with minimal configuration.
enrichment.seo_title: Dovel Review 2026: Self-Hosted SMTP Server Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovel is a free, open-source, Go-based SMTP server for self-hosting email. See 2026 features, setup, configuration, and how it compares to other options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dovel is a small, free, open-source SMTP server written in the Go programming language, built to let developers and system administrators self-host email sending and receiving with minimal setup. It was introduced publicly through a Show HN post on Hacker News in early 2023, where its creator described it as a way to self-host email in three steps, positioning it as a lightweight alternative to running a full-featured mail server stack for people who mainly want basic send and receive functionality under their own control.

Unlike most of the other tools in this comparison set, Dovel is not backed by a company, has no headquarters, employee count, or funding history; it is an independent open-source project maintained through public git repositories at dovel.email, distributed as a Go module (dovel.email/server) alongside related repositories for its mail-handling logic, hooks system, and site. The project is licensed under the GNU Lesser General Public License version 3 (LGPL-3.0), which permits free use, modification, and redistribution while requiring that changes to the licensed code itself remain open.

Technically, Dovel implements the core pieces needed to send and receive email over SMTP: it listens on standard mail ports (25 for traditional inbound SMTP, 587 as the modern TLS-encrypted submission port, and 465 as the older deprecated implicit-TLS port), supports TLS encryption for secure connections, and can be configured to serve multiple domains from a single instance. Configuration is intentionally minimal, stored in a single JSON file at ~/.config/dovel/config.json covering settings like the listening port, domain, timeouts, maximum message size, maximum recipients, and TLS certificate paths. Received and sent messages are processed through a hook system: Dovel executes user-defined scripts located in ~/.config/dovel/hooks/ (named receive-DOMAIN and send-DOMAIN) and pipes each message to that script in standard mbox format, which gives operators full flexibility to pipe mail into a local mailbox file, forward it elsewhere, or trigger custom processing logic rather than being locked into a fixed storage format. For outbound mail, Dovel supports per-user DKIM signing, which helps ensure messages sent from a self-hosted Dovel instance are less likely to be flagged as spam by receiving mail providers, and user accounts for sending are defined in a simple JSON array specifying a username, DKIM private key, and password. An optional web interface is available for browsing received email without needing a separate mail client.

Because Dovel is fully open source with no company behind it, there is no pricing structure of any kind: the software, its source code, and its documentation are entirely free to download, self-host, and modify under the LGPL-3.0 license. The only costs involved are whatever server or virtual machine a user chooses to run it on, plus the standard costs of owning a domain and setting up DNS records (including SPF, DKIM, and DMARC) required for reliable email deliverability, which is a manual, technical process regardless of which self-hosted mail server software is used.

Given its minimal feature set, lack of built-in IMAP support, and reliance on a hook-based scripting model rather than a graphical administration panel, Dovel is aimed almost exclusively at technically sophisticated users: individual developers, hobbyists, and security researchers who want a small, auditable, self-hosted SMTP component rather than a full mailbox-management solution, and who are comfortable writing their own shell scripts to handle message storage and routing. It is not designed to compete with full-featured self-hosted mail server projects such as Mailcow, Mail-in-a-Box, or Maddy, which bundle SMTP, IMAP, spam filtering, and webmail into a more complete, turnkey package; Dovel instead occupies a narrower niche as a minimal, dependency-light SMTP building block that developers can wire into their own infrastructure or scripts, more comparable in spirit to lightweight mail-sending libraries than to a full mail server product like Dovecot (which itself only handles IMAP/POP3, not SMTP).

Publicly available information about Dovel's adoption is limited: it shows modest engagement on package indexes and self-hosted software directories, with no widely reported user counts, and no major companies or case studies referencing production use of the project. This is consistent with its positioning as a niche, community-maintained developer tool rather than a widely adopted or commercially supported product, and readers evaluating Dovel should weigh its minimal feature set and small community against the deeper functionality and larger support ecosystems of more established self-hosted mail server projects.

The project's structure — separate but related repositories for the mail server core, the hooks system, and the project's own site, all published under the dovel.email namespace and distributed through Go's standard module tooling (go install dovel.email/server) — reflects a developer-first distribution model common to modern Go infrastructure tools, where installation is a single command rather than a packaged installer or configuration wizard. This is consistent with Dovel's target audience: engineers already comfortable with Go tooling, systemd or container-based deployment, and manual DNS configuration for SPF, DKIM, and DMARC records, rather than newcomers looking for a guided setup experience.

Within the broader landscape of self-hosted email software, Dovel sits at the far minimal end of the spectrum. Where turnkey projects like Mailcow or Mail-in-a-Box bundle a full IMAP server, webmail client, spam filtering, antivirus scanning, and an administrative web panel into a single Docker-based or scripted deployment, Dovel deliberately does none of that beyond SMTP send/receive and an optional lightweight web viewer, leaving mailbox storage, filtering, and long-term management to whatever hook scripts an operator chooses to write. This tradeoff makes Dovel easy to read and audit in full — the entire codebase is small enough for one developer to review end to end, a meaningful security property for a piece of software that touches email — at the direct cost of the batteries-included convenience that mainstream self-hosted mail projects offer out of the box. As of current public information, there is no indication of formal roadmap commitments, a paid support tier, or a company entity forming around the project, so prospective users should treat it as a single-maintainer open-source tool rather than a product with guaranteed long-term maintenance.

faqs.question: Is Dovel free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dovel is fully free and open source under the LGPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers or company behind it.

faqs.question: What is Dovel used for?
faqs.answer: Dovel is a lightweight, self-hosted SMTP server that lets developers send and receive email for one or more domains with minimal configuration, typically for personal or small-scale technical use cases.

faqs.question: Does Dovel support IMAP?
faqs.answer: No. Dovel focuses specifically on SMTP for sending and receiving mail; it does not include built-in IMAP support for full mailbox management.

faqs.question: What programming language is Dovel written in?
faqs.answer: Dovel is written in the Go programming language and distributed as a Go module.

faqs.question: Who maintains Dovel?
faqs.answer: Dovel is an independent, community-maintained open-source project rather than a product backed by a company; it was first shared publicly via a Show HN post on Hacker News in 2023.

faqs.question: How does Dovel handle received emails?
faqs.answer: Dovel uses a hook system that pipes each received or sent message, in mbox format, to a user-defined script, giving operators full control over how mail is stored or processed.
